content.problemStatement:
A Binod is a person who is very good with bitwise operations. Help Alice solve the following problem and become a Binod.

You are given an array AAA of NNN elements. Process QQQ queries on this array, of the following form:


Each query contains 555 integers k,L1,R1,L2,R2k, L_1, R_1, L_2, R_2k,L1‚Äã,R1‚Äã,L2‚Äã,R2‚Äã. It is guaranteed that L1‚â§R1<L2‚â§R2L_1 \leq R_1 \lt L_2 \leq R_2L1‚Äã‚â§R1‚Äã<L2‚Äã‚â§R2‚Äã.
The answer to a query is the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that:

L1‚â§i‚â§R1L_1 \leq i \leq R_1L1‚Äã‚â§i‚â§R1‚Äã and L2‚â§j‚â§R2L_2 \leq j \leq R_2L2‚Äã‚â§j‚â§R2‚Äã
Ai‚äïAjA_i \oplus A_jAi‚Äã‚äïAj‚Äã has its kkk-th bit set. Here ‚äï\oplus‚äï denotes the bitwise XOR operation.


**Note:** An integer xxx is said to have its kkk-th bit set if the (unique) binary representation of xxx contains 2k2^k2k. For example, 5=1012=20+225 = 101_2 = 2^0 + 2^25=1012‚Äã=20+22 has its zeroth and second bits set but not the first, while 16=100002=2416 = 10000_2 = 2^416=100002‚Äã=24 has only its fourth bit set.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ ‚Äî the number of elements in array and number of queries, respectively.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã.
The next QQQ lines describe queries. The iii-th of these QQQ lines contains 555 space-separated integers k,L1,R1,L2,R2k, L_1, R_1, L_2, R_2k,L1‚Äã,R1‚Äã,L2‚Äã,R2‚Äã ‚Äî the parameters described in the statement.




Output Format
For each test case, output QQQ lines.The iii-th of these lines should be the answer to the iii-th query.
constraints: The sum of NNN and QQQ over all test cases won't exceed 10510^5105 and 5‚ãÖ1055\cdot 10^55‚ãÖ105 respectively.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array is A=[1,2,4,3,2]A = [1, 2, 4, 3, 2]A=[1,2,4,3,2].

Query 111: the ranges are [1,3][1, 3][1,3] and [5,5][5, 5][5,5], and k=1k = 1k=1. There are three pairs of (i,j)(i, j)(i,j): (1,5),(2,5),(3,5)(1, 5), (2, 5), (3, 5)(1,5),(2,5),(3,5).

A1‚äïA5=1‚äï2=3=112A_1 \oplus A_5 = 1 \oplus 2 = 3 = 11_2A1‚Äã‚äïA5‚Äã=1‚äï2=3=112‚Äã has its first bit set
A2‚äïA5=2‚äï2=0=02A_2 \oplus A_5 = 2 \oplus 2 = 0 = 0_2A2‚Äã‚äïA5‚Äã=2‚äï2=0=02‚Äã doesn't have its first bit set
A3‚äïA5=4‚äï2=6=1102A_3 \oplus A_5 = 4 \oplus 2 = 6 = 110_2A3‚Äã‚äïA5‚Äã=4‚äï2=6=1102‚Äã has its first bit set
So, the answer is 222.


Query 222: the ranges are [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and [3,5][3, 5][3,5], and now k=2k = 2k=2. This time, there are 666 pairs of indices. Of them, it can be verified that (1,3)(1, 3)(1,3) and (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3) are the ones that satisfy the given condition.
